title: A trustworthy evidence trail: replication, reproducibility, and transparency

objectives.statement: Distinguish preregistration, open methods, replication, computational reproducibility, and sensitivity analysis by the evidence question each addresses.
Evaluate what safeguard evidence raises or lowers confidence in a bounded claim without treating agreement, disagreement, or transparency as proof.
Design a transparent verification plan that preregisters decisions, supports responsible inspection, uses an informative new-data test, and examines plausible analytic alternatives.

scenes.title: When a result leaves a trail
scenes.contentHtml:
When a result leaves a trail

A fictional plant-science lab reports that a mineral supplement made bean seedlings taller. The headline sounds simple. The path to it is not.

The researchers chose when to stop collecting observations. They decided which seedlings counted, which measurement summarized growth, and which analysis to show. Another team could try the same recipe, but only if the recipe is complete. Even then, one repeat would not turn the claim into a fact beyond doubt.

The useful question is not just “Was the result positive?” It is “What trail lets another person test how much confidence the result deserves?”

This lesson builds that trail from four safeguards:

  1. Preregistration records the important question and analysis decisions before the outcomes are known.
  2. Open methods make the procedure and analytic workflow inspectable, with appropriate protection for people and sensitive data.
  3. Replication tests the claim with new data.
  4. Sensitivity analysis asks whether the conclusion changes under other plausible assumptions or analytic choices.

They do different jobs. None is a certificate that a claim is true. Together they make hidden flexibility, missing detail, narrow conditions, and fragile conclusions easier to detect.

Activate what you know about bias

Before reading further, answer from memory:

A researcher tries several defensible outcome definitions after seeing the data, then reports only the definition that gives the largest difference. What made the result vulnerable to bias: the existence of several definitions, the timing of the choice, or the incomplete report?

All three matter, but in different ways. Several defensible definitions create researcher degrees of freedom. Choosing after seeing the result lets that flexibility favor a desired story. Reporting only the winning choice hides the dependence from the reader.

If that chain is hard to reconstruct, use this recovery route before continuing:

  • Write the scientific question.
  • List one choice that could change its answer.
  • Mark whether the choice was made before or after outcomes were visible.
  • Ask whether a reader can see the alternatives and their consequences.

That four-line audit is enough prerequisite knowledge for this lesson. You do not need to finish another resource first.

Your destination

By the end, you will be able to inspect a study description, assign each safeguard its proper job, and design a transparent verification plan. You will also know where the safeguards stop: preregistration can preserve a poor plan, open files can expose an error without correcting it, a replication can disagree for several reasons, and a sensitivity analysis is only as informative as the alternatives it tests.

Source Note
claims claim-four-safeguards-complementary, claim-researcher-flexibility-bias, claim-preregistration-role, claim-open-methods-role, claim-replication-role, claim-sensitivity-rolesources source-reproducibility-consensus, source-replication-perspective, source-cos-preregistration, source-fda-sensitivity, source-multiverse-analysis

The sources support the distinct functions and limits of the four safeguards and the risk created when outcome-informed analytic choices are selectively reported. The plant-science opening, bias-audit wording, recovery route, and four-question teaching sequence are original. Terminology differs across fields, so the next scene declares the meanings used in this lesson.

scenes.title: Four questions for one claim
scenes.contentHtml:
Four questions for one claim

Scientific fields do not use replication, reproducibility, and related terms in exactly the same way. In this lesson, the verbs matter more than the labels:

  • Re-run: Can someone use the same data and documented computational steps to obtain the reported result? We call this computational reproducibility.
  • Repeat: Can someone collect new data in an informative test of the claim? We call this replication.

A computationally reproducible error is still an error. A replication that disagrees is still informative. Keep asking what was held the same, what changed, and what claim was tested.

1. What was fixed before the outcomes were known?

A preregistration is a dated research plan placed in a registry before the relevant outcomes are inspected. A useful plan states the question, primary outcome, sampling or stopping rule, exclusions, and main analysis precisely enough to distinguish planned tests from later exploration.

Preregistration does not forbid learning from surprises. If an instrument fails or an assumption becomes implausible, investigators can change course. They should identify what changed, when, and why. The safeguard is the visible timeline, not obedience to a plan that no longer makes scientific sense.

A Registered Report adds a publication process to that idea. A journal evaluates the question and proposed method before results are known and can offer in-principle acceptance. After the study, the journal checks adherence, justified changes, and the quality of the conclusions. Exploratory findings can still be reported when clearly distinguished.

2. Can another person inspect what was done?

Open methods means reporting enough procedural and analytic detail for another person to understand and, where feasible, repeat the work. Depending on the study, the inspectable trail may include materials, protocols, instruments, variable definitions, analysis code, software versions, and data.

“Open” does not always mean “download everything without conditions.” Human-participant data may require de-identification, data-use agreements, or controlled access. Consent, law, and the risk of re-identification still apply. A clear route to appropriately protected access can be more responsible than a public file.

3. Does the claim survive a new-data test?

A replication collects new data to test a claim from prior evidence. No repeat is literally identical: time, samples, investigators, and settings change. A useful replication therefore states which parts were kept close to the original and which parts changed.

Agreement generally raises confidence that the claim survives the changed conditions. Disagreement generally lowers confidence in the claim as stated, but it does not by itself show fraud, incompetence, or that one study is the sole “true” result. It can expose chance variation, an error, a poorly described method, or a boundary on where the effect occurs.

4. Does the conclusion survive plausible alternatives?

A sensitivity analysis repeats the analysis for the same scientific question while changing a consequential assumption or analytic choice. Examples include defensible ways to handle missing observations or plausible model forms. The alternatives should be justified, not selected because they produce a preferred answer.

If the conclusion is similar across the tested alternatives, it is robust to those alternatives. If it changes materially, report the dependence instead of choosing the most convenient result. A multiverse analysis extends this logic by examining many reasonable analytic paths. It can reveal which choices matter, but judgment is still required to decide which paths are reasonable.

The diagram is a map, not a sequence. You do not have to finish one safeguard before thinking about another. Here is the same information without a visual layout:

Safeguard The audit question What it can expose What it cannot guarantee
Preregistration What was fixed before outcomes were known? outcome-guided switching, undisclosed stopping or exclusions a good question, valid measurements, or correct analysis
Open methods Can another person inspect what was done? missing procedural detail, coding choices, inaccessible evidence that the disclosed work is error-free
Replication Does the claim survive an informative new-data test? chance findings, method dependence, limits across samples or settings final proof or a unique explanation for disagreement
Sensitivity analysis Does the conclusion survive plausible alternatives? dependence on assumptions or defensible analytic choices robustness to alternatives that were never tested
Check
id check-same-or-new-data

Commit an answer before continuing: a second analyst receives the original data and code, rebuilds the environment, and obtains the reported estimate. Which question has been answered, and which has not?

The analyst has shown computational reproducibility for that result: the same inputs and documented steps produced the same output. No new data were collected, so the claim has not yet been replicated.

Source Note
claims claim-terminology-convention, claim-preregistration-role, claim-registered-reports-role, claim-open-methods-role, claim-privacy-boundary, claim-replication-role, claim-sensitivity-role, claim-multiverse-rolesources source-reproducibility-consensus, source-replication-perspective, source-cos-preregistration, source-cos-registered-reports, source-nih-data-privacy, source-fda-sensitivity, source-multiverse-analysis

The sources support the declared terminology, safeguards, process descriptions, and limits. The four-question map, table wording, retrieval prompt, and examples are independently synthesized. The Registered Reports source describes a publication model; policies and implementation can vary by journal. Sensitivity findings apply only to the alternatives examined.

scenes.title: Follow a seedling claim through four checks
scenes.contentHtml:
Follow a seedling claim through four checks

The following study, numbers, laboratories, and results are fictional teaching material. They are not evidence about a real supplement.

Worked Example
id worked-example-seedling-traillabel One claim, four different checks

1. State the claim to be checked.

A campus lab asks whether adding supplement S to a standard growth medium increases day-14 height in one bean cultivar under specified greenhouse conditions. Its preregistered large-effect claim is that the mean increase is at least +1.0 cm.

The claim is deliberately bounded. It does not say that S improves every plant, every measure of growth, or every growing condition.

Before collecting the replication data, the teams also define how that new-data test will bear on the large-effect claim. A 95% interval whose upper end is below +1.0 cm will count against that claim; an interval that includes zero will leave the direction unresolved; and an interval above zero but below +1.0 cm will support a smaller positive effect under the tested conditions. These teaching rules make the comparison diagnostic rather than treating two bare point estimates as verdicts. They do not replace scrutiny of design quality or the wider evidence.

2. Record the before-results decisions.

Before outcomes are visible, the team registers:

  • the primary outcome: day-14 height in centimetres;
  • the standard and supplemented conditions;
  • the cultivar, growth period, and greenhouse settings;
  • the sample-size and stopping rule;
  • the rule for excluding a damaged measurement;
  • the main comparison, 95% interval, and planned way to handle missing day-14 measurements;
  • the replication criteria for interpreting the large-effect claim.

This plan does not make those choices correct. It makes their timing inspectable. If a sensor failure later requires a different rule, the team can explain the deviation and label the revised analysis.

3. Leave an inspectable trail.

The team deposits the protocol, supplier and batch information, allocation procedure, measurement instructions, variable definitions, raw observations, analysis script, and software environment. A second analyst can now trace how each recorded height became the reported estimate.

The planned analysis reports a mean difference of +1.8 cm, with a fictional 95% interval from +1.1 to +2.5 cm, for S compared with the standard medium. A second analyst uses the same files and obtains the same estimate and interval. That is a successful computational re-run. It has not produced replication evidence because no new seedlings were measured.

4. Test plausible assumptions without changing the question.

Four day-14 heights are missing. The preregistered analysis handles them under one stated assumption. The team also uses two other defensible missing-data assumptions aimed at the same day-14 comparison.

Analysis of the same fictional study Estimated difference for S
Preregistered handling of missing heights +1.8 cm
Plausible alternative A +0.9 cm
Plausible alternative B +0.2 cm

The result is computationally reproducible, yet the size of the estimated difference depends on how the missing measurements are handled. The honest report shows that range and names the assumptions. It does not display only +1.8 cm or call the conclusion robust without qualification.

5. Collect new data.

Another lab follows the disclosed method with new seeds from the stated cultivar and reports +0.4 cm, with a fictional 95% interval from +0.1 to +0.7 cm. This is a replication because it uses new data to test the bounded claim. Because the interval's upper end is below the prespecified +1.0 cm threshold, the result counts against the large-effect claim while remaining compatible with a smaller positive effect under the tested conditions. How much confidence should change still depends on the quality and precision of both studies and the wider evidence. The result does not, by itself, tell us whether the difference from the original estimate arose from sampling variation, a hidden procedural detail, seed batches, greenhouse conditions, or a problem in one of the studies.

6. Calibrate the conclusion.

A defensible summary is:

In this fictional example, the reported estimate and interval can be reproduced from the original files, but the point estimate is sensitive to plausible handling of missing observations. The new-data estimate of +0.4 cm falls within the original study's +0.2 to +1.8 cm sensitivity range, and its interval is below the prespecified threshold for a large effect. These independent checks do not establish one combined effect size, but together they show that +1.8 cm should not be treated as a stable estimate. The evidence supports, at most, a smaller positive effect under the tested conditions and does not support a confident claim of a large, condition-independent effect.

Notice what the summary does not say. It does not declare S effective because the first estimate was positive. It does not discard the first result because the repeat was smaller. It reports what each check changed about confidence.

Why the four checks cannot substitute for one another

Suppose the second analyst finds a mislabeled variable in the open code. That discovery matters even if a new-data replication later agrees. Suppose both analyses of the first data set re-run perfectly, but every plausible missing-data assumption gives a different estimate. The files are reproducible; the conclusion is fragile. Suppose a replication disagrees but the original method omitted the temperature cycle. Openness might reveal why the two tests were not as similar as intended.

Each safeguard produces a different kind of evidence:

  • Preregistration reveals the planned path and later deviations.
  • Open methods allow inspection and re-use of the path.
  • Replication follows a new-data path.
  • Sensitivity analysis compares plausible analytic paths for the same question.

Fade one part of the reasoning

For each event, name the safeguard and write one sentence about what it changes. Commit before checking the guide.

  1. An analyst gets +1.8 cm from the original files.
  2. The estimate ranges from +0.2 to +1.8 cm under justified missing-data assumptions.
  3. A second lab measures new seedlings and gets +0.4 cm, with a 95% interval from +0.1 to +0.7 cm.
  4. The original team explains that a sensor failure forced a deviation from its registered exclusion rule.

A complete response says:

  1. Computational reproducibility: the disclosed inputs and steps regenerate the reported estimate, but this does not test new data.
  2. Sensitivity analysis: the estimate depends on a plausible assumption, so the uncertainty should appear in the conclusion.
  3. Replication: the new-data interval lies below the prespecified +1.0 cm large-effect threshold, so it weakens that claim while remaining compatible with a smaller positive effect. The strength of belief change still depends on study quality and wider evidence.
  4. Preregistration with transparent deviation: the dated plan shows what changed; the explanation lets a reader judge the change instead of pretending it was planned.

If you swapped events 1 and 3, circle the words original files and new seedlings. If you treated event 2 as a hunt for the best number, replace “Which answer wins?” with “How dependent is the conclusion on reasonable choices?”

Source Note
claims claim-four-safeguards-complementary, claim-preregistration-role, claim-open-methods-role, claim-replication-role, claim-sensitivity-rolesources source-reproducibility-consensus, source-replication-perspective, source-cos-preregistration, source-fda-sensitivity

The sources support the four types of check and the calibrated interpretation. All study details, numbers, tables, deviations, and reasoning prompts in this scene are original fictional teaching material; they should not be generalized to real supplements or plant biology. The sensitivity interpretation is limited to the alternatives examined, and replication disagreement can have several explanations.

scenes.title: Put the shortcuts under stress
scenes.contentHtml:
Put the shortcuts under stress

Start without looking back. Write the four audit questions from memory. Then attach one word to each question: before, inspect, new data, and alternatives.

A complete reconstruction is:

  1. What was fixed before outcomes were known? — preregistration
  2. Can another person inspect what was done? — open methods
  3. Does the claim survive new data? — replication
  4. Does the conclusion survive plausible alternatives? — sensitivity analysis

If you remembered the terms but not the questions, practise the questions. They tell you what evidence to look for.

One answer supplied

A research team publicly posts its protocol, variable definitions, raw non-sensitive measurements, analysis code, and software versions. A second analyst uses those files and regenerates the published table.

This demonstrates computational reproducibility for the table because the same inputs and steps produced the same output. It also shows one benefit of open methods. It does not demonstrate replication because nobody collected new data.

Complete the audit

For each fictional case, name the missing or tested safeguard and state one limit.

A. The late registration

A team examines all outcomes on Monday and uploads its “preregistration” on Tuesday without saying that the outcomes were already known.

This record cannot establish which decisions preceded those outcomes. Registration after seeing the relevant results is not preregistration for that analysis. The team can still report the work honestly as exploratory and disclose the timing.

B. The new sample

Another laboratory uses a sufficiently detailed protocol to repeat the test with new specimens. Its result is smaller and in the opposite direction.

This is a replication result. It lowers confidence in an unqualified original claim, but it does not identify one certain cause of disagreement. Compare samples, implementation, measurement, analysis, and random variation before proposing explanations.

C. The protected records

A human-participant study publishes its protocol, variable dictionary, analysis code, and a description of its de-identification process. Row-level data are available only through a reviewed request and data-use agreement because combinations of variables could reveal participants.

This can be a responsible form of transparency, not an automatic failure of openness. A reader should be able to see what exists, why access is controlled, who may request it, and which conditions apply. Public access must not override consent, law, or privacy.

D. The changing target

A primary analysis asks whether a treatment changes day-14 height. An “alternative analysis” instead asks whether leaves are darker at day 30.

That is a new outcome and a new question, not a sensitivity analysis of the day-14 height claim. Sensitivity analysis changes a consequential assumption or analytic choice while keeping the target question aligned.

Repair two tempting models

Misconception
id misconception-transparency-certifies-truth

Commit first. A preregistered study has open files and fully reproducible code. Must its scientific conclusion be correct?

Tempting model: enough transparency badges certify a result.

Why it fails: the plan could ask a poor question, the measurement could miss the intended concept, and reproducible code could repeat a mistake.

Repair: treat safeguards as evidence about a trail. Ask what each one makes inspectable and what uncertainty remains.

Recheck: open code reveals that two category labels were reversed. Openness helped find the error; it did not prevent it.

Misconception
id misconception-replication-is-verdict

Commit first. A replication agrees with the original. Does that prove the effect is real and general?

Tempting model: replication success proves truth, while disagreement proves falsehood or misconduct.

Why it fails: every replication changes some conditions, and both agreement and disagreement must be interpreted against sampling variation, method quality, and the claim's scope.

Repair: use directional confidence language. Agreement generally raises confidence across the tested changes; disagreement generally lowers confidence in the claim as stated or helps locate a boundary.

Recheck: three close repeats agree in one laboratory and a deliberately broader repeat disagrees in another setting. The combined evidence may support a narrow claim while weakening a broad one.

Faded practice

For each case below, write three lines: safeguard, evidence gained, remaining limit. No template words are supplied this time.

  1. A registered plan names one primary outcome, but a broken instrument forces a replacement measure. The report marks the change and gives the reason.
  2. Twelve reasonable data-processing combinations produce estimates ranging from nearly zero to a large difference.
  3. A new team follows the same core procedure in a different season and finds a similar result.

Use this guide only after committing:

  1. The preregistration provides a time-stamped comparison point; the disclosed deviation preserves the actual history. The replacement measure may still change what the study tested.
  2. A multiverse-style sensitivity analysis shows that processing choices strongly affect the estimate. The result does not say which path is best, and it cannot test omitted alternatives.
  3. Replication with new data raises confidence across the changed team and season. It does not prove the claim in every season or setting.

If your three answers all said “the study is valid,” replace that verdict with a specific change in confidence. If your sensitivity answer chose the largest estimate, report the distribution instead. If your replication answer ignored what changed, name the changed condition and narrow the conclusion.

Source Note
claims claim-four-safeguards-complementary, claim-terminology-convention, claim-preregistration-role, claim-open-methods-role, claim-privacy-boundary, claim-replication-role, claim-sensitivity-role, claim-multiverse-rolesources source-reproducibility-consensus, source-replication-perspective, source-cos-preregistration, source-nih-data-privacy, source-fda-sensitivity, source-multiverse-analysis

The sources support the distinctions, privacy boundary, and calibrated interpretations used in the cases. Every scenario is fictional and independently written. The multiverse is presented as an extension of sensitivity reasoning, not as a mechanical guarantee that every reasonable analysis was included.

scenes.title: Build a verification plan in a new setting
scenes.contentHtml:
Build a verification plan in a new setting

A fictional bench-science team reports that light schedule L increases the day-10 biomass of one algal strain in its laboratory setup. You have only the claim, not the team's planning record, files, or follow-up evidence.

Before reading the model, design four checks. For each one, state what a person would do and what the result would allow them to conclude.

One defensible transfer

Preregister the decision trail. Before day-10 biomass is known, register the bounded question, strain and growth conditions, primary outcome and time point, assignment and stopping rules, exclusions, missing-measurement plan, and primary comparison. Record later deviations rather than rewriting the plan.

Open the method trail. Share the light timing and intensity specification, culture and measurement protocol, material identifiers, variable dictionary, observations, analysis code, and software environment. If any information cannot be released, name what is withheld, why, and how appropriate access can be requested.

Plan a new-data test. Ask a second team to collect new cultures using the disclosed core method. State in advance which features should stay close and which change is intentional—such as a new operator or equipment unit—so the result tests a defined version of the claim.

Plan sensitivity checks. Identify plausible alternatives that still address day-10 biomass, such as justified handling of failed readings or another defensible model for the same comparison. Report how the conclusion varies across them instead of selecting the preferred estimate.

The four plans still do not answer whether L increases biomass. They specify evidence that would make the claim easier to audit, repeat, and calibrate.

Mastery check

The assessment contains four untimed, retakeable items worth twelve points. Attempt each item before opening its answer and reasoning.

Mastery means at least 9 of 12 points and at least one point on each item. That combination requires you to distinguish re-running from repeating, interpret sensitivity and replication together, protect participants while supporting inspection, and design all four safeguards in a new context.

If you miss an item, use its specific feedback:

  • For same-data versus new-data confusion, circle the data source.
  • For a sensitivity error, restate the unchanged scientific question and list only justified alternatives.
  • For an openness error, separate inspectability from unrestricted public release.
  • For a transfer gap, write the four audit questions first and fill one concrete action under each.

Then retry with the alternate scenario described in the answer record rather than memorizing a phrase.

Synthesis

Carry this compact map into later reading:

Before: Was the important path recorded before outcomes were known?
Inspect: Can another person understand and re-run what was done, within ethical access limits?
New data: Does an informative repeat raise or lower confidence in the bounded claim?
Alternatives: Does the conclusion depend on plausible assumptions or analytic choices?

The best conclusion is not the strongest sentence the evidence permits. It is the most accurately calibrated sentence: clear about what survived, what changed, and what remains uncertain.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every relationship in the diagram is repeated in the four-row table and in the before–inspect–new data–alternatives text map. The numeric results in the worked example appear in a table and are interpreted in sentences. No task requires color, dragging, hovering, audio, image recognition, speed, laboratory access, or disclosure of personal information.

You may answer in prose, bullets, or a table. You may type, dictate with your own device, or use assistive technology. The ordered questions provide the same learning route if the concept map is unavailable. In print, read the scenes in their numbered order; prompts always appear before their feedback.

Recovery route

If the four terms blur together, ignore the labels and sort evidence into three bins: same data, new data, or decision timing. Same-data regeneration is computational reproducibility; new-data testing is replication; a before-outcomes record is preregistration. Then ask whether alternative assumptions changed the same question—that is sensitivity analysis.

If “open” sounds like “public without limits,” write two columns: needed for inspection and unsafe or unlawful to release. Add de-identification, controlled access, or a data-use agreement where appropriate, and explain the route.

If disagreement feels like a verdict, write at least two possible explanations and one scope boundary before changing confidence. Do not infer misconduct from disagreement alone.

If sensitivity analysis feels like result shopping, declare the alternatives because they are plausible before comparing their results. Report the spread, including inconvenient outcomes.

Source Note
claims claim-four-safeguards-complementary, claim-terminology-convention, claim-preregistration-role, claim-open-methods-role, claim-privacy-boundary, claim-replication-role, claim-sensitivity-rolesources source-reproducibility-consensus, source-replication-perspective, source-cos-preregistration, source-nih-data-privacy, source-fda-sensitivity

The sources support the safeguard map, ethical access boundary, and cautious confidence language. The algal case, mastery rule, accessibility routes, and recovery sequence are original course material. The four safeguards improve the evidence trail but do not replace sound design, measurement, analysis, or scientific judgment.

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable. The baseline science dossier contains ten accepted fragments from isolated, science-only Claude CLI calls; each machine-readable modelUsage included claude-fable-5 and also claude-haiku-4-5-20251001. The schema permits one author record per accountable principal, so the ten sessions are preserved separately—not collapsed into a false run—in ATTRIBUTION.md with every prompt digest. Those calls did not receive this repository, Premed, the graph outcome ID, or the packaging prompt. Opus-routed outputs were excluded before packaging. Material editorial and pedagogical assistance used Codex by OpenAI, model gpt-5.6-sol, Codex CLI 0.144.6, run 12858cbe-5233-4bc4-ae6c-25e3879f5a5e. Digests cover supplied material prompts, not hidden provider or system instructions.

items.prompt: A second analyst receives the original observations, code, and environment description and obtains the reported estimate. No new specimens are measured. Which interpretation is best?

options.text: The result is computationally reproducible from the supplied files, but the claim has not been replicated with new data.
The claim has been replicated and is now proven because two analysts obtained the same estimate.
The sensitivity analysis is complete because the original code ran twice.
The study is preregistered because its methods can now be reconstructed.

answer.summary: Same-data regeneration is computational reproducibility, not new-data replication.
answer.reasoning: The analyst used the original inputs and documented computational steps, so the result can be regenerated from those files. Replication requires an informative test with newly collected data. Running the code twice does not compare plausible analytic alternatives, and reconstructing a method after outcomes are known does not create a before-outcomes preregistration. For a retry, imagine a new analyst rebuilding an old weather analysis: circle whether the data are old or newly collected before naming the safeguard.
commonErrors: Calling every repeated action a replication without checking whether the data are new.
Treating successful code execution as proof that the scientific conclusion is correct.
Confusing a recoverable workflow with a preregistered decision timeline.

items.prompt: Interpret the three records together. Name the safeguard represented by each record and write a calibrated conclusion about the size and stability of the claim. Do not choose one estimate as the winner.
items.stimulus: Fictional materials study: before results, the team defines a large stable difference as at least 8 units and specifies that a replication 95% interval with an upper end below 8 will count against that claim. The preregistered analysis reports 12 units, with a 95% interval from 9 to 15. A second analyst obtains the same result from the original files. Three justified analyses for the same target question produce point estimates of 2, 11, and 13 units. A second laboratory measures new samples and reports 3 units, with a 95% interval from 1 to 5.

answer.correct: The 12-unit re-run shows computational reproducibility for the original files. The 2-to-13-unit range is a sensitivity result showing dependence on plausible analytic choices. The 3-unit new-sample result is a replication; its 1-to-5 interval lies below the prespecified 8-unit threshold, so it counts against the large stable-difference claim without proving no difference. The replication point estimate also falls inside the sensitivity range. These distinct checks both warn against treating 12 as a stable estimate, but they do not identify one combined effect size or explain the variation by themselves.
answer.summary: The files re-run, while sensitivity and a prespecified new-data comparison both count against a large stable difference.
answer.reasoning: Each record answers a different question. Regenerating 12 and its interval from the same inputs checks the computational trail. The spread from 2 to 13 checks how the same target conclusion depends on justified analytic alternatives. The new laboratory's estimate tests the claim with new data, and its 1-to-5 interval lies below the prespecified 8-unit large-effect threshold. The replication point estimate of 3 also lies within the sensitivity range of 2 to 13. That connection shows that a smaller estimate was already possible under a justified analysis of the original data, but it does not make sensitivity analysis and replication interchangeable or reveal why results vary. The appropriate conclusion reports reproducibility and both sources of caution; it does not average the values without a model, infer a zero effect, or treat one study as a final verdict. For a retry, substitute a fictional sound-measurement study with a prespecified large-effect threshold of 6, same-file 8 with interval 7 to 9, sensitivity values 1 to 9, and a new-sample value of 2 with interval 1 to 4, then repeat the three labels and one-sentence calibration.
acceptableResponses: Any answer that correctly labels same-file regeneration, justified alternative analyses, and new-sample testing, then uses the prespecified threshold and interval to limit confidence in a large robust effect.

An answer may say that the evidence is compatible with a smaller effect or a result dependent on conditions, provided it notes that the replication estimate lies within the sensitivity range and does not claim proof, no effect, or misconduct.
commonErrors: Calling the same-file re-run a replication.
Reporting only the preregistered or largest estimate and ignoring the sensitivity range.
Treating a difference between bare point estimates as decisive without using the prespecified threshold and interval.
Treating the smaller replication estimate as proof that the original investigators erred or acted improperly.

items.prompt: A human-participant survey publishes its protocol, variable dictionary, and analysis code, but row-level records have a meaningful re-identification risk. Explain why unrestricted public release is not required for responsible transparency, and name two concrete features the team should provide instead.

answer.correct: Transparency must remain consistent with consent, law, and participant privacy. The team can describe de-identification and residual risk, publish metadata and code, and provide a documented controlled-access request process with eligibility rules and a data-use agreement. Any two concrete protective and inspectable features earn the corresponding credit.
answer.summary: Responsible openness can use de-identification and controlled access rather than unrestricted release.
answer.reasoning: Open methods aim to make the evidence trail inspectable; they do not override duties to participants. Public row-level data can permit re-identification through combinations of variables. A useful record explains what is available, what is withheld and why, how de-identification was handled, and how qualified users can request access under enforceable conditions. For a retry, imagine precise wildlife-location records whose release could aid poaching: write what can be public and what needs a protected access route, while noting that the ethical basis differs from participant privacy.
acceptableResponses: Consent and privacy can justify controlled access; provide a variable dictionary plus a reviewed request route and data-use agreement.
Describe de-identification and residual risk, publish code and aggregate or synthetic examples, and document how qualified researchers can apply for protected access.
commonErrors: Saying that transparency always requires posting every row publicly.
Withholding the existence and structure of the data without explaining an access path.
Naming privacy without proposing any inspectable documentation or controlled-access mechanism.

items.prompt: A fictional team claims that coating C reduces water loss from cut plant stems during a defined four-hour bench test. Design a four-part verification plan: one preregistration action, one open-methods action, one informative replication, and one sensitivity analysis. For each part, state what it would reveal and one limit on the conclusion.

answer.correct: A complete response records the outcome, stopping and exclusion rules, and primary analysis before results; shares enough coating, stem, measurement, data, code, and environment detail for inspection; specifies a new-data repeat with stated same and changed conditions; and tests justified alternative assumptions or analyses for the same four-hour water-loss question. It explains that these actions expose timing, permit audit, test the bounded claim with new samples, and reveal dependence on choices, while none proves a universal or error-free effect.
answer.summary: A strong plan assigns a concrete action, evidential job, and limit to each of the four safeguards.
answer.reasoning: The four safeguards are complementary. Preregistration exposes when consequential decisions were made. Open methods make the physical and computational trail inspectable. Replication requires new stems and a defined relation to the original conditions. Sensitivity analysis keeps the target question fixed while varying justified assumptions or analytic choices. The conclusion must remain bounded to tested conditions and alternatives. For a retry, replace the coating with a fictional filter-material test and rebuild the plan from the four questions before naming the safeguards.
acceptableResponses: Plans may use different concrete variables or plausible alternatives if they preserve the fictional four-hour water-loss target and clearly distinguish same-data analysis from new-data replication.
A response may propose controlled rather than unrestricted access if it identifies a legitimate restriction and an inspectable access route.
commonErrors: Listing safeguard names without a concrete action, evidential job, and limit.
Calling a second analysis of the original observations a replication.
Changing the target outcome in the sensitivity analysis.
Claiming that the four safeguards prove the coating works for all stems or settings.

rubrics.title: Distinguish same-data regeneration from new-data replication

criteria.description: Selects computational reproducibility and explicitly preserves the boundary that no new-data replication occurred.

rubrics.title: Interpret reproducibility, sensitivity, and replication together

criteria.description: Correctly identifies same-file regeneration, justified analytic alternatives, and a new-sample test.
Uses the sensitivity range to identify dependence on plausible analytic choices rather than selecting a preferred estimate.

Uses the prespecified threshold and replication interval, notes that the replication estimate lies within the sensitivity range, and writes a bounded conclusion without claiming proof, zero effect, or misconduct.
Explains that consent, law, and re-identification risk can rule out unrestricted row-level release.
Names a concrete documentation or protection feature, such as a de-identification description, variable dictionary, or residual-risk statement.
Names a concrete inspectable access route, such as reviewed controlled access with eligibility rules and a data-use agreement.

rubrics.title: Design and bound four complementary safeguards

criteria.description: Provides a before-results registration action and explains what its timing reveals and what it cannot guarantee.
Provides concrete procedural and analytic materials for inspection and states a remaining error or access limit.
Uses new samples in an informative repeat, states what is held close or changed, and bounds the inference.
Tests justified alternative assumptions or analytic choices for the same target question and reports dependence rather than a preferred result.

sources.title: An international consensus on core reproducibility items in research
sources.version: PLOS Biology 24(4): e3003726; republished April 24, 2026; acknowledgment correction published May 7, 2026
sources.locator: Abstract; Box 1; Table 1; Methods reproducibility and computational reproducibility items. The correction changes acknowledgments, not the cited definitions.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used to verify terminology variation and the roles of sufficiently described methods, code, data, and analysis conditions. Lesson prose, definitions, examples, diagram, table, prompts, and sequence are independently written; no source expression or figure is reused.

agentAccess.notes: PLOS terms were checked before substantive article access. They provide open article access and article reuse terms and contained no prohibition on this bounded agent reading. The public article and correction were accessed without login, paywall, batch retrieval, or access-control bypass.

sources.title: What is replication?
sources.version: PLOS Biology 18(3): e3000691; commissioned Perspective; not externally peer reviewed
sources.locator: Article type and editorial note; introduction; sections 'What is replication?' and 'Replication is about the outcome, not the intentions'; conclusion

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used to verify the same-data versus new-data distinction and the diagnostic, non-binary interpretation of replication results. All lesson expression, fictional cases, prompts, and reasoning are original; no source example, figure, or wording is reused.

agentAccess.notes: PLOS terms were checked before the public article was read and no restriction on this bounded agent access was found. Access used the public article route without authentication, paywall, automated bulk download, or access-control bypass.

sources.title: Preregistration
sources.locator: Sections 'What is preregistration?', 'Why preregister?', and guidance on changes, confirmatory analyses, and exploratory analyses

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used to verify preregistration as an advance plan, the separation of planned and exploratory work, and disclosure of deviations. Lesson wording, teaching structure, examples, and prompts are independently created; no source expression is reused.

agentAccess.notes: The OSF/COS public terms, updated January 10, 2025, were checked before the initiative page. They describe public website and API use and contained no agent-ingestion prohibition applicable to this bounded reading. No login, bulk collection, or technical-control bypass was used.

sources.title: Registered Reports
sources.locator: Overview and process description for Stage 1 review, in-principle acceptance, Stage 2 review, and clearly labeled exploratory analyses

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used to verify the general two-stage Registered Reports process. The lesson avoids absolute claims about eliminating bias, notes that journal implementation varies, and reuses no source language, visual, example, or organization.

agentAccess.notes: The OSF/COS public terms were checked before the substantive page and no applicable agent-ingestion restriction was found. The page was read through ordinary public access without login, scraping, rate-limit evasion, or access-control bypass.

sources.title: E9(R1) Statistical Principles for Clinical Trials: Addendum: Estimands and Sensitivity Analysis in Clinical Trials — Guidance for Industry
sources.locator: Sections II and V.B; Section VI, especially pages 19–22; Glossary definition of sensitivity analysis
sources.license: U.S. Government work; public domain except where otherwise noted

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used to verify that sensitivity analyses examine robustness to departures from assumptions and should remain aligned to the target question or estimand, with key analyses planned and assumptions documented. The lesson generalizes only this methodological principle and independently creates all language and examples.

agentAccess.notes: FDA website policies were checked before the guidance. They identify most FDA website content as public domain unless noted and contained no agent-ingestion restriction for this access. The official public PDF was read without login, automation at scale, or access-control bypass.

sources.title: Principles and Best Practices for Protecting Participant Privacy
sources.locator: Operational Principles; 'Apply Appropriate De-identification Measures'; controlled-access repositories and data use agreements
sources.license: U.S. Government work; public domain unless otherwise noted

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used to verify that responsible data sharing considers consent, law, de-identification, residual privacy risk, controlled access, and data-use agreements. No NIH wording, checklist, media, or case is reproduced; all course expression and scenarios are original.

agentAccess.notes: NIH public-site reuse information was checked before the guidance and states that most NIH public website content is public domain unless noted. No agent-ingestion restriction was found. The current public guidance was read without login, bulk collection, or technical-control bypass.

sources.title: Increasing Transparency Through a Multiverse Analysis
sources.locator: Abstract; introduction on data-processing choices and selective reporting; section 'The multiverse analysis'; Discussion

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used to verify multiverse analysis as systematic examination of reasonable data-processing choices, result dependence on those choices, and the selective-reporting concern when one preferred path is shown without the alternatives. The lesson uses only facts in original language and does not reuse the source example, analysis, data, figure, or expressive structure.

agentAccess.notes: SAGE journal terms were checked before substantive source access. The terms include text-and-data-mining conditions and no prohibition applicable to this bounded factual reading was found. Access used public bibliographic and article routes without login circumvention, bulk download, or technical-control bypass.

claims.statement: Preregistration, open methods, replication, and sensitivity analysis address different weaknesses in an evidence trail and should be interpreted as complementary safeguards rather than certificates that a scientific claim is true.
claims.scope: Introductory scientific-inquiry model for assigning distinct evidential jobs and limits to the four safeguards across research fields.
claims.uncertainty: Fields use reproducibility terminology differently, and the value of each safeguard depends on the design, implementation, and claim being examined.

claims.statement: Defensible design and analysis choices can produce different results; choosing among them after seeing outcomes and reporting only a preferred result can conceal that dependence and make the reported result vulnerable to bias.
claims.scope: Researcher flexibility in data processing and analysis, including selective presentation of one result from multiple reasonable paths.
claims.uncertainty: Having legitimate choices does not itself establish bias or misconduct; the concern arises from outcome-informed selection and incomplete reporting, and which choices are defensible depends on context.

claims.statement: In the declared convention for this lesson, computational reproducibility means obtaining a reported result from the same input data and documented computational steps or conditions, whereas replication uses newly collected data in an informative test of a claim.
claims.scope: A declared operational vocabulary for separating same-data computational regeneration from new-data scientific tests.
claims.uncertainty: The labels replication and reproducibility are used differently across disciplines; the lesson therefore foregrounds the actions re-run and repeat.

claims.statement: A useful preregistration records consequential research and analysis decisions before the relevant outcomes are known, distinguishes planned from exploratory work, and remains compatible with justified changes when deviations are disclosed.
claims.scope: General preregistration practice for making the timing and status of planned, changed, and exploratory analyses visible.
claims.uncertainty: Preregistration quality varies; a dated plan does not guarantee a sound question, valid measurement, adequate design, or correct analysis.

claims.statement: Registered Reports commonly add peer review of the question and proposed method before outcomes are known, possible in-principle acceptance, and a later check of adherence, justified deviations, and conclusions while allowing clearly labeled exploration.
claims.scope: The general two-stage Registered Reports publication model described by the Center for Open Science.
claims.uncertainty: Journal policies and implementation details vary; this process description is not a guarantee that every Registered Report is bias-free or published regardless of all quality concerns.

claims.statement: Sufficiently described methods, materials, variables, code, data where appropriate, software, and analytic conditions allow others to inspect and sometimes re-run or repeat a study, but disclosure alone does not make the work error-free.
claims.scope: Methods and computational transparency sufficient to understand, inspect, and where feasible regenerate or repeat reported work.
claims.uncertainty: What must be shared depends on the study and may be limited by privacy, legal, ethical, proprietary, or feasibility constraints that require explicit documentation.

claims.statement: Responsible sharing of human-participant data must account for consent, law, de-identification, and residual re-identification risk; controlled access and data-use agreements can support qualified inspection when unrestricted public release is inappropriate.
claims.scope: U.S. NIH-guided privacy principles for sharing data from research participants; used here to establish an ethical boundary on open data.
claims.uncertainty: Applicable law, consent language, repositories, institutional decisions, and risk assessments vary by jurisdiction and dataset; the lesson does not provide legal advice.

claims.statement: A replication uses new data to test a defined claim; agreement generally raises confidence across the tested changes, while evidence against the defined claim generally lowers confidence or helps identify error or scope boundaries without uniquely proving misconduct or falsehood. Interpreting the result requires the claim, design, precision or uncertainty, and wider evidence rather than a comparison of bare point estimates alone.
claims.scope: General interpretation of informative new-data tests of bounded scientific claims.
claims.uncertainty: No replication is exact, labels such as direct and conceptual form a continuum, and the strength of belief change depends on prior and existing evidence as well as sampling variation, precision, implementation, measurement, analysis, and changed conditions.

claims.statement: Sensitivity analysis examines whether a conclusion for the same target question or estimand changes under justified alternative assumptions or analytic choices; stability supports robustness only to the alternatives tested, while instability should be reported as dependence.
claims.scope: The general robustness logic of sensitivity analysis, abstracted from formal clinical-trial estimand guidance for introductory scientific inquiry.
claims.uncertainty: The FDA source is clinical-trial guidance; disciplines vary in methods, and an analysis cannot establish robustness to unexamined or unjustified alternatives.

claims.statement: A multiverse analysis systematically examines many reasonable data-processing or analytic paths to reveal how results depend on those choices, while the set of paths remains a reasoned and potentially incomplete judgment.
claims.scope: Multiverse analysis as an extension of sensitivity reasoning about reasonable analytic choices.
claims.uncertainty: Reasonable specifications are context-dependent; a multiverse does not mechanically include every defensible analysis or select the scientifically best model.

terms.definition: A dated research plan recorded before the relevant outcomes are known so planned decisions can be distinguished from later changes and exploration.
The set of choices available when designing, measuring, processing, analyzing, and reporting a study, some of which can change the result.
An analysis developed after patterns or outcomes are inspected, used to generate or refine ideas and reported as exploratory rather than as a test fixed in advance.
A publication format in which a journal evaluates the research question and proposed method before results are known and later evaluates execution, justified changes, and conclusions.
Procedural and analytic information made sufficiently inspectable for another person to understand and, where feasible and permitted, re-run or repeat the work.
Obtaining a reported computational result from the same input data and documented analysis steps, code, and relevant computational conditions.
An informative test of a scientific claim using newly collected data, with the relation between original and changed conditions made explicit.
A comparison of results for the same target question under justified alternative assumptions or analytic choices, used to show how dependent the conclusion is on those choices.
Stability of a conclusion across specified plausible changes in assumptions, analytic choices, data, or conditions; the relevant changes must always be named.
A systematic analysis across many reasonable data-processing or analytic paths to reveal which choices materially affect a result.
A protected route in which data are available only to approved users under stated eligibility, security, and use conditions rather than through unrestricted public release.
The possibility that records described as de-identified could still be linked to a person, especially by combining variables with other information.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson identity

  • Lesson: PREM-SCI-009 — A trustworthy evidence trail: replication, reproducibility, and transparency
  • Accountable principal: VSBDev (github:VSBDev)
  • Course-content licence: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)
  • Candidate state: version 0.1.0, draft, source confidence pending review

Blind science baseline

The accepted scientific first draft came from /tmp/fable-dossier-02-09.md. The dossier contains ten fragments generated in isolated Claude CLI calls that received science-only context. They did not receive this repository, Premed materials, the graph outcome ID, or the editorial-packaging prompt. Outputs routed through Opus were excluded before this pack was built.

Each accepted fragment's machine-readable modelUsage contains claude-fable-5 and also claude-haiku-4-5-20251001. The records below therefore preserve each run separately and do not claim that Fable was the only model involved. Lesson schema validation requires a unique accountable principal per authors entry, so lesson.json contains the one accountable principal with the material packaging run; the ten blind science sessions remain separately and fully disclosed here rather than being collapsed into one false actor run or represented as invented principals.

2 ca41b569-8674-4dd6-94d1-bb0dc5bab66d 5ec2b85c96bcf3f1ba4fd1e92090ecaa70e58b4391aed61de25f8e07f0f691fc claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
3 ac6bece5-7d81-4486-a8e9-492c92f0bdc6 a6e9754214e387b847e6e5fa0a8779c467f32c267a8ea6c53069bbeb2165794d claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001

5 b0b9068d-fef2-4279-8dc0-a3e52f1e2018 fbcfd363eeea8379c17bb031dcc97ebba0e711076906d629ce9a800eae5b6955 claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
6 7662e5f9-554e-4bea-9ab6-0c69c998bfce 5054a7632f06708a4c12c7d8856fafdf73805ae58db8f34e7891fc0da9bd0e54 claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
7 62d1dbab-bab0-4c58-8d22-4ab734627dce ec9696ba74123f22b167fb4f6da3fb8d39386d34608f8f65a980ceeb7c2b4e4e claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
8 fc24a2b2-01c1-4db5-9277-195b970e2e2e bf84bf17084575f9b6f426f491606412ca78fc761aa7c166db0bc31fc99c3dc7 claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
9 b66f4404-ca7c-402f-bc8d-2b45789ffcc1 5f478276482aad710ebb249bd610e1418f4f1d062a8837f322e1ba0c3f6e6bee claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
10 cc1b1638-7c80-4843-a965-80d21e205f2a 337d7a04da9147f7c17ca68ea471191411e152449ac18e408bb0745fc8bbd1de claude-fable-5; claude-haiku-4-5-20251001

The ten prompt digests above are the dossier-supplied hashes of the isolated science-fragment prompts; they do not cover hidden provider or system instructions. They are preserved in this required attribution artifact because the schema's single-principal author record cannot encode ten runs without creating duplicate or invented principals.

Material editorial and pedagogical assistance

VSBDev is accountable for the contribution. Material editorial and pedagogical assistance used Codex by OpenAI, model gpt-5.6-sol, version Codex CLI 0.144.6, in packaging run 12858cbe-5233-4bc4-ae6c-25e3879f5a5e.

The supplied packaging-prompt digest is sha256:4583365568c411a791f03975cf2a63963f5674529987b92d1f05d6c2322b9bc9, obtained from EK_PACKAGER_PROMPT_SHA256. It identifies the exact discloseable material packaging prompt supplied to this run and excludes hidden provider and system instructions.

The packager verified consequential claims, corrected overstatements, designed the four-question explanatory model, created the fictional worked example and transfer cases, built retrieval and fading support, authored the assessment and feedback, mapped claims and sources, created the declarative diagram, and prepared the typed pack. This record describes packaging assistance, not the blind science calls as having performed repository-aware lesson design.

Scientific editorial decisions

The accepted dossier's useful scientific substance was preserved where supported: same-data computational reproducibility, new-data replication, preregistration and Registered Reports, responsible openness, sensitivity analysis, multiverse analysis, and calibrated interpretation of replication results.

The pack makes three material corrections or exclusions:

  • It declares a local terminology convention because reproducibility and replication labels vary by field.
  • It replaces claims that a successful replication proves reliability or that safeguards remove false positives with directional, bounded confidence language.
  • It excludes the dossier's database-system replication passage because it is unrelated to the assigned scientific-inquiry outcome.

The specification-curve fragment was not developed into a separate learner concept because an accessible, permitted authoritative source was not needed once multiverse and general sensitivity analysis covered the bounded outcome. No unsupported detail from that fragment was retained.

Original synthesis and source use

The lesson's prose, four-question model, seedling and algal cases, numbers, tables, diagram, retrieval prompts, misconception repairs, transfer task, assessment, rubrics, feedback, accessibility routes, and recovery sequence are original course material. The sources in references.json were used only to verify factual and methodological claims. No source wording, expressive organization, example, question, figure, media, or dataset was copied, translated, adapted, or redistributed.

The PLOS consensus source was checked against its May 7, 2026 correction. That notice changes acknowledgments introduced during proofing, not the cited methodological content.

Source-access attestation

Before substantive access on 2026-07-21, the applicable public terms routes were checked for PLOS, Center for Open Science/OSF, FDA, NIH, and SAGE. Each included source is recorded as checked-no-agent-restriction-found; no login, paywall, rate-limit evasion, bulk collection, or technical access-control bypass was used.

Two candidate routes were deliberately excluded:

  • National Academies substantive content was not processed for the lesson after its current terms disclosed restrictions relevant to automated and AI use.
  • Springer Nature's specification-curve article was not used after its AI guidance did not provide a suitable basis for this workflow.

These excluded routes do not appear in references.json and support no lesson claim.

Third-party assets

No third-party image, diagram, audio, video, dataset, or other asset is included. diagrams/four-safeguard-questions.diagram.json is an original declarative representation authored for this pack. All numeric study results are explicitly labeled fictional teaching material.

Post-review finalization

VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable for the single post-review revision and adjudication. Material finalizer assistance used run codex-finalizer-PREM-SCI-009-6039609A-483F-4729-B36E-2E36D2A7B67A under discloseable-instructions digest sha256:be27f517ba2c2c955ecd76d527a026c546086f575737698a6409d9faf522efd3. Runtime system, provider, model, and version are recorded as RUNTIME-STAMPED in adjudication.json for operator substitution from the runtime banner.

The finalizer independently revised the original fictional quantitative cases, their answer logic, the researcher-flexibility claim mapping, source classification, and pacing estimate in response to the two preserved reviews. These changes reuse no third-party expression, media, or dataset.

Governance boundary

The original candidate made no claim of approval, publication, merge readiness, independent verification, clinical sufficiency, or course credit. The preserved reviews and final adjudication are separate governance records. A merge adjudication value is an evidence-based recommendation, not a claim that GitHub merged or published the lesson.
